1:
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָם לֶךְ-לְךָ — "and the LORD said to Abram, 'go for yourself.'" Lēḵ-lĕḵā — the doubled imperative with the reflexive/emphatic dative suffix (-lĕḵā, "for you/yourself") carries both a command to go and an implication that the going is for Abram's own ultimate benefit and fulfillment. The same construction appears at Genesis 22:2 (go to Moriah) — the two lēḵ-lĕḵā commands bracket the Abraham narrative as its opening and climactic test.

The three objects Abram must leave are expressed in three increasingly intimate concentric circles: מֵאַרְצְךָ (mēʾarṣĕḵā, from your land — largest, most general), וּמִמּוֹלַדְתְּךָ (ûmimmôlaḏtĕḵā, from your kindred/relatives), וּמִבֵּית אָבִיךָ (ûmibbet ʾāḇîḵā, from your father's household — smallest, most intimate). The movement is from general to particular — from geography to bloodline to immediate family. God demands the severance of all three layers of identity before naming the destination.
2:
וְאֶעֶשְׂךָ לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל (wĕʾeʿeśĕḵā lĕḡôy gāḏôl) — "and I will make you into a great nation." Gôy (nation, political entity) is the word used for all the nations in Genesis 10:5, 20, 31-32 — all the gôyim from the Table of Nations are here promised to be matched and exceeded by the one gôy that will come from Abram. Seven divine promises follow (counted differently by scholars, but conventionally seven): (1) I will make you a great nation; (2) I will bless you; (3) I will make your name great; (4) you will be a blessing; (5) I will bless those who bless you; (6) whoever curses you I will curse; (7) in you all the families of the earth will be blessed. The sevenfold structure mirrors the seven days of creation — a new creative act is beginning.

וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ — "and I will bless you." Bārak (Piel, to bless) — the blessing formula that appeared in creation (Genesis 1:22, 28), in Noah (Genesis 9:1), and now in Abram. שְׁמְךָ גָּדוֹל — "your name great" — the reversal of Babel (Genesis 11:4: "let us make a name for ourselves"). What humanity tried to achieve by city-building and tower-construction, God gives freely to the one who obeys the call to leave.
3:
וַאֲבָרְכָה מְבָרְכֶיךָ — "I will bless those who bless you." The reciprocal blessing/curse principle: those who bless Abram will be blessed; וּמְקַלֶּלְךָ אָאֹר — "whoever curses you I will curse" — the singular form (mĕqallĕlĕḵā is singular) for the cursing vs. the plural for blessing (mĕbārĕḵeḵā), possibly emphasizing that blessing is expected to be widespread but cursing rare.

וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה — "and in you all the families of the earth will be blessed." Wĕnibrĕḵû — the key form: Niphal (passive) third-person plural imperfect. The Niphal reading (passive: they will be blessed) is supported by the MT vocalization and is the reading Paul uses in Galatians 3:8 ("in you shall all the nations be blessed"). An alternative vocalization gives Hitpael (reflexive: they will bless themselves by you/call themselves blessed by you, as in Genesis 18:18 and 22:18 where the form is different). The MT passive reading supports Paul's Christological application: the blessing flows through Abram to all nations, not merely that nations will invoke Abram's name in self-blessing.
4: וַיֵּלֶךְ אַבְרָם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֵלָיו יְהוָה — "so Abram went, as the LORD had spoken to him." The obedience formula — as with Noah (Genesis 6:22; Genesis 7:5; Genesis 7:9) — signals total and immediate compliance. Hebrews 11:8 comments: "By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out to a place that he was to receive as an inheritance. And he went out, not knowing where he was going" — the narrative does not tell Abram his destination before he leaves, only that God will show him the land (Genesis 12:1: "the land I will show you"). The going precedes the knowing.
5: אֶת-כָּל-רְכוּשָׁם אֲשֶׁר רָכָשׁוּ וְאֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ בְחָרָן — "all the possessions they had accumulated and the people (nepeš) they had acquired in Haran." Nepeš here in the sense of persons/slaves — "the souls they had made/acquired." ʿĀśû (they made/acquired) applied to persons: either the slaves and servants accumulated as wealth (standard ANE household servants), or possibly (rabbinic reading) converts/proselytes made in Haran. The word nepeš keeps the human-dignity dimension: these are living persons, not mere property, even if legally held as servants.
6:
אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה (ʾēlôn môreh) — "the oak/terebinth of Moreh." Môreh = teacher, oracle-giver (from yārāh, to teach, to direct). The oak of Moreh at Shechem (Šĕḵem) was likely a divination site — a sacred tree associated with the receiving of oracles or instruction. Abram's first stop in Canaan is at a pagan sacred site, where YHWH appears to him (Genesis 12:7). God meets Abram at the very location where the Canaanites sought their gods — the LORD's theophany at a Canaanite holy site is itself a territorial claim.

וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי אָז בָּאָרֶץ — "and the Canaanite was then in the land." The parenthetical note acknowledges the obvious difficulty: the land God is promising to Abram's descendants is occupied. The note is not an explanation but an observation — the promise is made in full knowledge of the existing inhabitants.
7: וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָם (wayyērāʾ YHWH ʾel-ʾabrām) — "and the LORD appeared to Abram." Nîrāʾ (Niphal perfect, lit. "was seen" or "appeared") — the theophany verb used for all patriarchal appearances of God (Genesis 17:1; Genesis 18:1; Genesis 26:2, 24; Genesis 35:1, 9; Genesis 46:29; Genesis 48:3). The form indicates that God made himself visible to Abram — the nature of the appearance is not described, but it is an objective visible event, not merely an inner impression. לְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת — "to your offspring (zeraʿ) I will give this land." The land-promise is to the zeraʿ (seed/offspring) — the same collective-or-singular word that carried the promise in Genesis 3:15. The land promise is to Abram's descendants, not yet to Abram personally.
8: וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — "and he built there an altar to the LORD." Altar-building punctuates Abram's journey through the land: Genesis 12:7 (at Shechem, after the first theophany), Genesis 12:8 (at Bethel), Genesis 13:18 (at Mamre/Hebron), Genesis 22:9 (at Moriah). Each altar marks a site of divine encounter and Abrahamic worship — Abram is marking the land with worship of YHWH, establishing a counter-presence to the Canaanite cult sites. וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה — "and he called on the name of the LORD" — the worship formula introduced at Genesis 4:26 (Enosh's generation began calling on the LORD's name). Abram continues this line of YHWH-worshippers in Canaan.
9: וַיִּסַּע אַבְרָם הָלוֹךְ וְנָסוֹעַ הַנֶּגְבָּה — "Abram journeyed on, going and setting out toward the Negev." The infinitive absolute construction hālôḵ wĕnāsôaʿ (going and journeying = journeying progressively, continually moving) describes a steady southward migration. The Negev (hanneḡeḇ, the dry south) is the semi-arid region between Judah and the Sinai — Abram is moving from central Canaan toward the southern borderlands before the Egyptian episode.
10: וַיְהִי רָעָב בָּאָרֶץ — "now there was a famine in the land." The famine (rāʿāb) is the narrative pressure that drives Abram into Egypt — the land of promise cannot yet sustain its future heir. The same pressure (famine → Egypt) will drive Jacob's family to Egypt in Genesis 42:5; Genesis 43:1 — the Abram/Egypt episode is a structural anticipation of the Exodus narrative. לָגוּר שָׁם — "to sojourn (gûr) there" — gûr is the word for resident-alien sojourning, the status of someone living outside their native land without full citizen rights (cf. Genesis 17:8; Genesis 20:1; Genesis 21:23, 34; Genesis 26:3 — Abram consistently sojourns rather than owning the land).
11: הִנֵּה-נָא יָדַעְתִּי כִּי אִשָּׁה יְפַת-מַרְאֶה אָתְּ — "behold, I know that you are a beautiful woman in appearance." Yĕpat-marʾeh (beautiful of appearance/sight) — the standard biblical beauty idiom; cf. Genesis 26:7 (Rebekah) and Genesis 29:17 (Rachel). Abram's speech to Sarai reveals his fear before the fact: he plans the deception before it is necessary. Yāḏaʿtî (I know) — the knowing is intimate and certain; Abram is under no illusion about the danger his wife's beauty creates.
12: וְהָרְגוּ אֹתִי וְאֹתָךְ יְחַיּוּ — "they will kill me and let you live." The calculus is stark: Abram's life vs. Sarai's freedom. The wife-sister ruse (repeated in Genesis 20:1-18 with Abimelech and in Genesis 26:6-11 with Isaac and Rebekah) is Abram's default survival strategy — one that places Sarai in danger to preserve himself. The text presents this without direct authorial condemnation, but the narrative consequence (Pharaoh's righteous indignation in Genesis 12:18-19; the divine plagues in Genesis 12:17) functions as implicit moral assessment.
13: אִמְרִי-נָא אֲחֹתִי אָתְּ — "say, please, that you are my sister." The request uses the particle nāʾ (please, I beg you) — indicating deference/request rather than command. In Genesis 20:12, Abram acknowledges that Sarai is indeed his half-sister (daughter of the same father, different mother), so the statement is technically true while functionally deceptive. The use of a partial truth to create a false impression raises the question of whether Abram's action is acceptable self-protection or cowardly deception — the narrative's non-resolution of this question is itself a literary feature.
14: כִּי-יָפָה הִוא מְאֹד — "for she was very beautiful." Mĕʾōḏ (very, greatly) — the same intensifier as the creation's "very good" (Genesis 1:31). Sarai's beauty is extreme enough to be noticed by Egyptians immediately. The Egyptians' observation (wayirĕʾû) leading to official commendation (Genesis 12:15) follows quickly — the plan Abram feared comes true within verses of his warning to Sarai.
15: וַיְהַלְלוּ אֹתָהּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה (wayĕhallĕlû ʾōtāh ʾel-parʿōh) — "and they praised her to Pharaoh." Hālal (Piel, to praise, commend, boast of) — the root of hallĕlûyāh (praise YHWH). Officials who see Sarai perform the same verb as worship: they extol her beauty to the king. Parʿōh (Pharaoh) — the Hebrew rendering of the Egyptian royal title pr-ʿȝ (great house), used for all Egyptian kings in the OT regardless of dynasty. Sarai is taken into the royal harem — the word bêt parʿōh (Pharaoh's house) is a term for the royal household/palace.
16: וּלְאַבְרָם הֵיטִיב בַּעֲבוּרָהּ — "and he dealt well with Abram for her sake." The material benefit Abram sought (Genesis 12:13: "that it may go well with me for your sake") is realized: sheep, cattle, donkeys, servants (ʿăbāḏîm wəšipḥōt), female donkeys, and camels. The material enrichment of Abram from the Egyptian sojourn (continued in Genesis 13:2: "Abram was very rich") parallels and anticipates the Exodus: Israel will be enriched by the Egyptians as they leave (Exodus 12:35-36). The Egypt-sojourn typology runs deep.
17: וַיְנַגַּע יְהוָה אֶת-פַּרְעֹה נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים — "and the LORD afflicted Pharaoh with great plagues." Nāgaʿ (Piel, to strike, afflict) + nĕgāʿîm (plagues, afflictions) — the same root for the Exodus plagues (nāgaʿ in Exodus 11:1; the noun nega appears throughout Leviticus for skin diseases/impurities). The word-pair nāgaʿ/nĕgāʿîm creates an explicit etymological link between this mini-Exodus and the actual Exodus — Pharaoh afflicts Israel, God afflicts Pharaoh, in both the proto-Exodus (Genesis 12) and the Exodus proper. The divine protection of Sarai (and thus the promise-line) is enacted without Abram's initiative or moral worthiness.
18: מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לִּי — "what is this you have done to me?" Pharaoh's rhetorical question exactly mirrors God's question to the woman after the Fall (Genesis 3:13: "what is this you have done?") and to Cain after the murder (Genesis 4:10: "what have you done?"). The pagan ruler's moral indignation uses the same formula as divine judicial inquiry — the irony is rich: the one who should be the least morally sophisticated is asking the question most associated with divine judgment. Pharaoh's rhetorical position is ethically superior to Abram's in this moment.
19: לָמָּה אָמַרְתָּ אֲחֹתִי הִוא — "why did you say 'she is my sister'?" The threefold rhetorical accusation (mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā lî, Genesis 12:18; lāmmāh ʾāmartā, Genesis 12:19a; lāmmāh lōʾ higgaḏtā lî, Genesis 12:18) builds through repetition. Pharaoh's "now then, here is your wife" (wĕʿattāh hinnēh ʾištĕḵā) restores Sarai with a formal directive — the pagan king acts with more honor than the patriarch. The narrative does not record Abram's response to this indictment — his silence is as theologically significant as Pharaoh's words.
20: וַיְצַו עָלָיו פַּרְעֹה אֲנָשִׁים — "Pharaoh gave orders about him to his men." The expulsion of Abram from Egypt with his wife and possessions intact parallels and anticipates the Exodus: Israel leaves Egypt with Egyptian goods (Exodus 12:35-36), escorted out by Pharaoh's command. The proto-Exodus that began with Abram's descent to Egypt in famine (Genesis 12:10), the affliction of Pharaoh's household (Genesis 12:17), and the departure with wealth (Genesis 12:20; Genesis 13:2) provides the narrative template for the full Exodus of Israel's descendants centuries later. Abram is Israel in miniature.
